channel.title: Code Defence Intelligence Brief — Tue, 24 Mar 2026
channel.description: Daily cybersecurity intelligence for Tue, 24 Mar 2026.

item.title: Citrix urges immediate patching of critical NetScaler data leak flaw. 🛡️
item.description:
Your gateway is leaking sensitive session data to unauthenticated attackers. 🛡️
CVE-2026-3055 · Severity 9.3 · Critical Memory Overread in NetScaler ADC & Gateway.
@[Citrix](urn:li:organization:1598) has released emergency security updates to address a critical vulnerability in NetScaler ADC and NetScaler Gateway. The flaw stems from insufficient input validation‚ allowing an unauthenticated remote attacker to perform a memory overread and leak sensitive information from the application process memory.
Leaked data can include session tokens‚ administrative credentials‚ and internal network configuration. This is a maximum-priority risk for organizations using Citrix for remote access‚ as session hijacking can bypass multi-factor authentication entirely.
The uncomfortable truth: Your perimeter security tools are the most attractive targets for attackers because they often possess the highest level of trust and access to your internal network.
→ Update NetScaler ADC and NetScaler Gateway to the latest patched releases immediately. → Force a global password reset for all administrative accounts that have logged into the Gateway in the last 48 hours. → Monitor for unusual session activity originating from non-standard or geographic IP blocks.
#Cybersecurity #NetworkSecurity #Citrix #PatchManagement #CISO #CodeDefence

item.title: TeamPCP compromises official Checkmarx GitHub Actions workflows. 🐛
item.description:
Your automated security tools are being weaponized to steal your corporate secrets. 🐛
Supply Chain Alert · TeamPCP hijacking official @[Checkmarx](urn:li:organization:230873) GitHub Actions.
The hacking group TeamPCP has successfully compromised two official @[Checkmarx](urn:li:organization:230873) GitHub Actions: ast-github-action and kics-github-action. By using stolen CI credentials‚ the attackers have modified the official release tags to distribute malicious code designed to exfiltrate build environment variables and developer secrets.
This follows the group recent supply-chain attack on the Trivy scanner. Because these actions are used by thousands of organizations to secure their code‚ the compromise of these tools provides a direct path for the attacker to move into the heart of the corporate development pipeline.
The uncomfortable truth: Your automated security scanners are only as secure as the @[GitHub](urn:li:organization:19041) Actions that deliver them; a single hijacked tag can turn your defense tool into a malware delivery vehicle.
→ Audit your CI/CD pipelines for any use of Checkmarx actions and verify the integrity of the commit hashes. → Immediately rotate all secrets‚ tokens‚ and API keys that may have been exposed in GitHub Actions logs over the last 48 hours. → Implement pin-to-hash policies for all third-party GitHub Actions to prevent unauthorized tag hijacking from impacting your builds.
#Cybersecurity #SupplyChain #DevSecOps #GitHub #Checkmarx #CodeDefence

item.title: CISA warns of actively exploited Wing FTP Server vulnerability. 🐚
item.description:
Your internal file transfer configurations are being leaked to unauthenticated scanners. 🐚
CVE-2025-47813 · Severity Medium · Active Exploitation in Wing FTP Server.
The @[CISA](urn:li:organization:13010360) has added an information disclosure flaw in Wing FTP Server to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog today. This vulnerability allows an unauthenticated attacker to leak the installation path and configuration data through crafted requests.
While rated as medium severity‚ this is a critical precursor for path traversal and RCE attacks. Attackers are using these leaks to map internal directories before launching destructive secondary payloads.
The uncomfortable truth: Information leaks in your perimeter services are the building blocks for full administrative takeovers.
→ Update Wing FTP Server to the latest security release immediately to prevent directory leakage. → Restrict management and FTP ports to known internal IP ranges using hardware firewalls. → Audit server logs for anomalous GET requests targeting system-level configuration files.
#Cybersecurity #Infosec #NetworkSecurity #ZeroTrust #SOC #CodeDefence

item.title: North Korean StoatWaffle malware targets developers via VS Code tasks. 🤖
item.description:
North Korean threat actors are using the automated features of developer tools to bypass security controls. 🤖
Threat Alert · StoatWaffle malware abusing Visual Studio Code tasks.json feature.
A North Korean hacking group is distributing the StoatWaffle malware via malicious @[Microsoft](urn:li:organization:1035) Visual Studio Code ❨VS Code❩ projects. The attack leverages the tasks.json file‚ which is designed to automate build and test tasks. When a developer opens the compromised project and triggers the auto-run feature‚ the malware is deployed silently.
This tactic is particularly dangerous because developers often work with external or open-source projects without a full security review of the project configuration. The malware is designed for data theft and initial access persistence.
The uncomfortable truth: The tools designed to increase your developers productivity are the same ones that attackers use to bypass your most expensive endpoint security controls.
→ Educate developer teams specifically on the risks of opening unverified VS Code projects and the abuse of tasks.json. → Use EDR tools to monitor for unusual shell activity originating from IDE processes. → Audit your network for anomalous outbound connections originating from developer workstations.
#Cybersecurity #DevSecOps #VSCode #NorthKorea #AppSec #CodeDefence
